text: Hello and welcome to This Is My Architecture. Today I'm joined by Josh from Ookla. Hi Josh.
Hi, thank you for having me. So let's start off. Can you tell me a little bit about what Ookla does?
Yeah, so Ookla's flagship product is Speedtest, which is the best way for consumers to measure
their internet connection. Awesome. Well, I know I've used it before. I'm sure many people watching
have used it as well, but I'm really excited to hear more about the architecture we have
today. So can you dive in for us? Yeah, absolutely. So as users take Speedtests, we receive reports
about their internet experience to an API. We receive about 4,000 of those per second.
Those come from consumers to our API that drops them into a Kinesis stream. This Kinesis stream
really enables us to have an async architecture from here on out. We also get 24 hours of data
retention in that Kinesis stream. So if we have any slowdowns or issues further in the pipeline,
we can always catch up. So we have the data retained in Kinesis. Awesome. So why Kinesis?
Yeah, so Kinesis gives us a lot of robust scale opportunity. We previously used to do a lot of work
inline in the API connecting to RDS, that sort of thing. And this has allowed us to scale far beyond
what we could do with that system. That's great. So once we're in Kinesis, where does the data flow
from there? Yeah, so we then hit our report enrichment step, which is where we build links between the
measurement data that we see in these reports and fact tables that we have in RDS. So these facts might
be things like the mobile carrier or the device manufacturer or the geographic place that we
want to associate these measurements with. Oh, that's great. So we have the data flowing to RDS here,
and I see we have Kinesis Firehose up there. So what happens from there? Yeah, so once we've enriched
these reports with the dimensions from the fact tables, we write those enriched JSON reports to Kinesis
Firehose. Kinesis Firehose gathers several thousand of those and compresses them and writes them to as
newline separated JSON into our S3 data lake. So I'm guessing with 4000 reports per second, how much data
do we have in that data lake? Yeah, so we have a little over five petabytes of data uncompressed in
that data lake. Wow, that's a lot. So the data moves to the data lake. Where does it go from there?
Yeah, so after the data lake, we really want to pull through to our data warehouse, which is Redshift,
for our further aggregation and processing. To do that, we use a Lambda job. It's triggered on new
objects landing in the data lake. That Lambda job normalizes the data into the CSV that we can pull into
Redshift tables. And so down here with Redshift, do we have this data flowing to customers or is this
for internal use? Yeah, so Redshift really gives us the the power to run aggregates over long time frames
and come up with the the insights and analysis that we want to write back to RDS and ultimately expose
back to our customers through the Speedtest Intelligence product. So I know that we have some
near real-time data happening. Is there any way to go back and use some past data?
Yeah, so we actually we use Athena to explore the data, the full breadth of data that we have
collected in the data lake. It allows us to write SQL queries and analyze any field in the JSON that
we've collected. Once we've identified a new piece of data or new analysis that we'd like to do and
expose to our customers, we are able to actually trigger this Lambda job retroactively on the historical
data in the data lake. We can go back six months or a year and pull those insights through and it's as
if we've been collecting them for a year. Yeah, so we have a lot of data coming in. We also see a lot of
serverless happening in here too, which is awesome. This must be really great for your customers to have
the ability to get this past data near real-time data and then provide these insights. So thanks so much
for joining us, Josh. I've learned a lot and thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
